question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|猿も木から落ちる|saru mo ki kara ochiru|SA-roo mo KI ka-ra o-CHI-roo}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Prefer practical benefits over appearances.
Get two benefits from one action.
Have a narrow view because you know only a small world.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Even experts make mistakes.'. Word-to-word: "even monkeys fall from trees."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|花より団子|hana yori dango|HA-na yo-ree DAN-go}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Get two benefits from one action.
Have a narrow view because you know only a small world.
Keep getting back up after setbacks.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Prefer practical benefits over appearances.'. Word-to-word: "dumplings rather than flowers."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|一石二鳥|isseki nichou|is-SE-kee nee-CHO}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Get two benefits from one action.
incorrectAnswers: Have a narrow view because you know only a small world.
Keep getting back up after setbacks.
Different people have different tastes.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Get two benefits from one action.'. Word-to-word: "one stone, two birds."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|井の中の蛙大海を知らず|i no naka no kawazu taikai o shirazu|ee no NA-ka no ka-WA-zoo tai-KAI o shee-RA-zoo}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Have a narrow view because you know only a small world.
incorrectAnswers: Keep getting back up after setbacks.
Different people have different tastes.
Small things add up over time.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Have a narrow view because you know only a small world.'. Word-to-word: "a frog in a well does not know the great sea."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|七転び八起き|nanakorobi yaoki|na-na-ko-RO-bee ya-O-kee}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Keep getting back up after setbacks.
incorrectAnswers: Different people have different tastes.
Small things add up over time.
Careless words can cause trouble.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Keep getting back up after setbacks.'. Word-to-word: "fall seven times, rise eight."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|十人十色|juunin toiro|JOO-neen TOY-ro}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: Small things add up over time.
Careless words can cause trouble.
A valuable thing is wasted on someone who cannot appreciate it.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Different people have different tastes.'. Word-to-word: "ten people, ten colors."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|塵も積もれば山となる|chiri mo tsumoreba yama to naru|CHEE-ree mo tsoo-mo-RE-ba YA-ma to NA-roo}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Small things add up over time.
incorrectAnswers: Careless words can cause trouble.
A valuable thing is wasted on someone who cannot appreciate it.
Good advice is falling on deaf ears.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Small things add up over time.'. Word-to-word: "even dust, when piled up, becomes a mountain."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|口は災いの元|kuchi wa wazawai no moto|koo-CHEE wa wa-za-WAI no MO-to}}' most naturally mean?
incorrectAnswers: A valuable thing is wasted on someone who cannot appreciate it.
Good advice is falling on deaf ears.
Chasing too many goals means you may catch none.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Careless words can cause trouble.'. Word-to-word: "the mouth is the source of disaster."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|猫に小判|neko ni koban|NE-ko nee ko-BAN}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: A valuable thing is wasted on someone who cannot appreciate it.
incorrectAnswers: Good advice is falling on deaf ears.
Chasing too many goals means you may catch none.
The safest route is often the fastest in the end.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'A valuable thing is wasted on someone who cannot appreciate it.'. Word-to-word: "gold coins to a cat."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|馬の耳に念仏|uma no mimi ni nenbutsu|OO-ma no MEE-mee nee nen-BOO-tsoo}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Good advice is falling on deaf ears.
incorrectAnswers: Chasing too many goals means you may catch none.
The safest route is often the fastest in the end.
Follow local customs when you are in a new place.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Good advice is falling on deaf ears.'. Word-to-word: "a Buddhist chant into a horse's ears."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|二兎を追う者は一兎をも得ず|nito o ou mono wa itto o mo ezu|NEE-to o O-o MO-no wa EET-to o mo E-zoo}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: Chasing too many goals means you may catch none.
incorrectAnswers: The safest route is often the fastest in the end.

Patience and perseverance pay off.
explanation: This idiom is used in everyday japanese for the idea 'Chasing too many goals means you may catch none.'. Word-to-word: "one who chases two rabbits catches not even one."

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|急がば回れ|isogaba maware|ee-so-GA-ba ma-WA-re}}' most naturally mean?
correctAnswer: The safest route is often the fastest in the end.

question: What does the Japanese idiom '{{ja-JP|郷に入っては郷に従え|gou ni itte wa gou ni shitagae|GO ni it-te wa GO ni shee-ta-GA-e}}' most naturally mean?

incorrectAnswers: Patience and perseverance pay off.
